Facts.text: A solar eclipse may have marked the return of Odysseus, legendary King of Ithaca, to his kingdom after the Trojan War
Masada, a Jewish fortress, falls to the Romans after several months of siege, ending the Jewish Revolt
Pelagius I begins his reign as Catholic Pope
Bari falls to Robert Guiscard's Norman forces, ending Byzantine rule in Italy
Franciscan envoys Giovanni da Pian del Carpine and Benedict to Pole depart Lyon on first Catholic mission to the Mongols. Carpine returns 1247 as first European with account of a Mongolian court
The Serbian Empire is proclaimed in Skopje at an Easter assembly and Stephen Uroš IV Dušan crowned Emperor over much of the Balkans
Azzo X d'Este is defeated at the Battle of Portomaggiore by Venetian-Ferrarese troops
Spanish conquistador Hernando de Lerma founds the settlement of Salta in Argentina
The University of Münster in North Rhine-Westphalia, Germany is founded
First American comedy play, "The Contrast," debuts in NYC
Spithead Mutiny begins: British Royal Navy sailors protest over living and working conditions and pay near Portsmouth
US Senate ratifies Rush-Bagot Agreement, limiting naval armaments on the Great Lakes
Giacomo Meyerbeer's opera "Le prophète" premieres in Paris
First passenger railway opens in India from Bori Bunder (Bombay) to Thane (34 km)
San Salvador is destroyed by an earthquake
Steamer "Long Beach" sinks off Long Beach, NY, 311 die
The Wernerian Natural History Society, a former Scottish learned society, is wound up
American Confederate Congress approves conscription act for all white males (18-35 years)
Slavery is abolished in the District of Columbia
Battle of Columbus & West Point, Georgia (Ft Tyler), Union forces win one of the last battles of the US Civil War
Nitroglycerine explodes at a Wells Fargo & Co office in San Francisco shattering glass for half a mile
Louisiana voters approve new constitution
Ebenezer Bassett, 1st African American diplomat, begins service as Minister to Haiti
Vaudeville Theatre Strand opens in London
In Dodge City, Kansas, Bat Masterson fights his last gun battle
Drentse & Friese peat cutters go on strike in the Netherlands
US Post Office issues its first stamp booklets containing 12, 24, or 48 two-cent stamps
Natural Bridges National Monument forms (Lake Powell, Utah)
Pirates turn a rare 5-3-7 doubleplay (left fielder covers 2nd base)
Bulgarians and the Turks agree to an armistice that will be accepted by the other nations involved
The British House of Commons passes a new Military Service Bill, taking men up to 55 years old and extending to Ireland
Liberal Freedom League forms in The Hague, Netherlands

Treaty of Rapallo between the German Republic and Soviet Union a is signed in Italy, normalizing diplomatic relations with each side renouncing their territorial and financial claims against the other
1st radio-transmission of wireless: Mattheus Passion
Child labor laws strengthened in Holland
During the Communist St Nedelya Church assault in Sofia, Bulgaria, 150 are killed and 500 wounded
Book of the Month Club sends out its 1st selections "Lolly Willowes" & "Loving Huntsman" by Sylvia Townsend Warner
Cleveland Indians become the first MLB team to permanently feature numbers on the backs of uniforms; numbers correspond to the position in the batting order
Cleveland rookie center fielderEarl Averill, becomes first American League player to hit a HR on 1st at bat; Indians beat Detroit Tigers, 5-4 at League Park
1st radio broadcast of "Fibber McGee & Molly"
Great Britain recognizes Italian annexation of Abyssinia
Stanley Cup Final, Boston Garden, Boston, MA: Boston Bruins beat Toronto Maple Leafs, 3-1 for a 4-1 series win; first best-of-7 SC Final series
Heitor Villa-Lobos' opera "Izaht" premieres in Rio de Janeiro
New York Yankees dedicate a plaque to Jacob Ruppert in Monument Park, Yankee Stadium
Little Theater at Adelphi Strand, London closes
Japanese occupying army on Java installs film censorship
40 NZ bombers attack Haarlem, Netherlands (85 killed)
Battle of Okinawa: US troops land and seize the Ie Shima Airfield on Ie Shima island near Okinawa
US troops enter Nuremberg, Germany
World War II: American troops liberate Colditz Castle, the high-security prisoner of war camp in Saxony, Germany
World War II: Dutch town of Arnhem, site of failed Operation Market Garden, is freed by British and Canadian forces
World War II: German troops in Groningen, Netherlands surrender to Canadian and Dutch allied forces
1st US launch of captured V-2 rocket, White Sands, New Mexicao; reaches altitude of 8 km
Dutch Special Court of Justice sentences Frederik Müller, Socialist mayor of Rotterdam, to 10 years in jail for his wartime support of Germany
-17] Explosions & fire on French ship Grandcamp

Fire aboard French-flagged cargo ship SS Grandchamp, docked at Port of Texas City, Texas and loaded with 2,300 tons of ammonium nitrate causes massive explosion and spreads to nearby ships and oil facilities; deadliest industrial accident in US history kills 581, including all but one member of the city's fire departments
Lens to provide zoom effects demonstrated (NYC)
Organization for European Economic Cooperation (OEEC) forms in Paris
Stanley Cup Final, Maple Leaf Gardens, Toronto, ON: 3-peat for Toronto Maple Leafs as they beat Detroit Red Wings, 3-1 for a 4 game series sweep
British submarine Affray sank in English Channel, killing 75
Philadelphia Phillies second baseman Connie Ryan bats 6 from 6 in a 14-12 defeat against the Pirates at Forbes Field, Pittsburgh
Stanley Cup Final, Montreal Forum, Montreal, Quebec: Montreal Canadiens beat Boston Bruins, 1-0 for a 4-1 series win
WAND TV channel 17 in Decatur, IL (ABC) begins broadcasting
KVAL TV channel 13 in Eugene, OR (CBS) begins broadcasting
Stanley Cup Final, Olympia Stadium, Detroit, MI: Detroit Red Wings beat Montreal Canadiens, 2-1 in OT for a 4-3 series victory
1st solar powered radios go on sale
Stanley Cup Final, Montreal Forum, Montreal, Quebec: Dickie Moore has a goal and 2 assists as Montreal Canadiens beat Boston Bruins, 5-1 for a 4-1 series victory
USSR performs atmospheric nuclear test at Semipalatinsk, Kazakhstan
French government of Félix Gaillard falls due to Tunisia crisis
Datu Abdul Rozak inaugurated as premier of Malaysia federation
MLB Philadelphia Phillies' Dave Philley gets a major league record 9th straight pinch hit, having ended the previous season with a streak of 8
15th Tony Awards: "Becket" (play) & "Bye Bye Birdie" (musical) win
Stanley Cup Final, Olympia Stadium, Detroit, MI: Chicago Blackhawks beat Detroit Red Wings, 4-1 for a 4-2 series victory
Brazil nationalizes US businesses
9 men sentenced 25-30 years for Britain's 1963 "Great Train Robbery"
Test flight of heavy Saturn S-1C-rocket
Rhodesian Prime Minister Ian Smith breaks diplomatic relations with Britain
70 die in a snow crush (France)
Apollo 16 launched; 5th manned lunar landing (Decartes Highlands)
Chicago Cubs rookie pitcher Burt Hooton hurls a 4-0 no-hitter against the Philadelphia Phillies at Wrigley Field, Chicago
Two British soldiers are shot dead by the Official Irish Republican Army (OIRA) in separate incidents in Derry
Two giant pandas arrive in US from China
USSR performs nuclear test at the Semipalatinsk Test Site in northeast Kazakhstan
Cambodian Red Khmer occupy Phnom Penh
NBC's premiere of miniseries "Holocaust"
Failed Palestinian attack on Zaventem Airport, Belgium
Sam Shepard receives Pulitzer Prize for "Buried Child"
Delhi beat Bombay by 240 runs to win Ranji Trophy final
US performs nuclear test at Nevada Test Site
Queen Elizabeth proclaims Canada's new constitution
Pulitzer prize awarded to Mary Oliver for "American Primitive"
West Indies complete 5-0 demolition of England
British Conservative MP Harvey Proctor appears at Bow Street Magistrates' Court in London charged with gross indecency
FCC imposes a broader definition of indecency over airwaves
Howard Stern and Infinity Broadcasting are warned by FCC regarding content
Peter Taylor's "Summons to Memphis" wins Pulitzer Prize for fiction
Berendrechtsluis opens in Antwerp, biggest flood lock in world
Costa Rica beats US 1-0, in 3rd round of 1990 world soccer cup
Zeleka Metaferia wins 3rd World Cup marathon (2:10:28)
Maximum NY State unemployment benefits raised to $260 per week
Supreme Court rejects appeal from retarded man, Dalton Prejean, condemned to death for murdering a Louisiana state trooper in 1977
Leander & Seago's musical "Matador" premieres in London
St Louis Blues becomes 8th NHL team in Play-off to come back from a 3-1 deficit as they beat the Detroit Red Wings 3-2 in game 7
1st concrete is poured at new ballpark at Gateway (Jacobs Field)
Afghan president Najibullah resigns
NY Rangers win team record 50th game
The Katina P runs aground off of Maputo, Mozambique. 60,000 tons of crude oil spill into the ocean.
Circus performers Marissa Young (24) & Matt Richardson (21) wed
Howard Stern Radio Show premieres in Minneapolis/St Paul, Minneapolis on WRQC 100.3 FM
Treaty of Accession is signed in Athens, admitting 10 new member states to the European Union
Washington Wizards'Michael Jordanplays his final NBA game, in Philadelphia, where he receives a 3 minute standing ovation.
The super liner Queen Mary 2 embarks on her first Transatlantic crossing, linking the golden age of ocean travel to the modern age of ocean travel.
Virginia Tech massacre: The deadliest mass shooting in modern American history. The gunman, Seung-Hui Cho, kills 32 people and injures 23 others before committing suicide.
At least 55 people are killed in the Syrian uprising despite UN presence to oversee ceasefire
For the first time since 1977 no Pulitzer Prize is awarded for fiction
16 people are killed after a gold mine collapses in Kyekyewere, Ghana
37 people are killed by a 7.8 magnitude earthquake in Khash country, Iran
Real Madrid defeat Barcelona to win its 19th Copa del Rey trophy
South Korean ferry MV Sewol sinks on route Incheon to Jeju, 304 drown, mostly students. National controversy erupts over rescue efforts and actions of crew and owner.
22 million Americans have filed for unemployment in 4 weeks (5.2 million in the last week), wiping out 9 1/2 years of job gains
Nationwide State of Emergency declared in Japan till 6 May due to the worsening COVID-19 outbreak
New study claims a multi-decade megadrought is already under way in the western US, published in journal "Science"
Spanish extreme athlete Beatriz Flamini exits a deep cave in Granada, Spain, after spending  500 days isolated underground in probable world record, as part of social isolation experiment

Sudan erupts into violence as street fighting and bombing raids strike Khartoum amid a power struggle between military generals al-Burhan and Hamdan, killing more than 83 people and injuring over 1,126
Denmark's historic stock exchange, the Børsen, dating from 1625 goes up in flames in Copenhagen while being renovated
United Arab Emirates records its largest rainfall in 75 years, flooding Dubai airport and killing at least 18 people in Oman
Volcano Mount Ruang erupts in Indonesia in North Sulawesi province, its first eruption since 2002

A juvenile colossal squid (11.8 inches long) is filmed in its natural environment at 1,968 feet deep for the first time since its discovery in 1925 near the South Sandwich Islands in the South Atlantic Ocean
Chinese chess grandmaster Ju Wenjun wins her 5th consecutive Women's World Chess Championship with a score of 6.5–2.5, becoming the first Chinese player to win the championship five times
The US Navy launches a wave of missile strikes across Houthi-controlled Yemen, killing at least 31 people and injuring 101 others
French army underLouis XIIenters the Alps
Martin Lutherarrives at the Diet of Worms assembly
Albrecht von Wallensteinappointed supreme military commander of the Holy Roman Empire
Queen Anneof England knights scientistIsaac Newtonat Trinity College, Cambridge
Battle of Culloden: Royalist troops under the Duke of Cumberland defeat the Jacobite army ofCharles Edward Stuartin the final battle of the Jacobite Rising on British soil
George Washingtonheads to his first presidential inauguration
Napoleonic Wars: The Battle of Mount Tabor -Napoleondrives Ottoman Turks across the River Jordan near Acre
Franz Liszt's symphonic poem "Mazeppa" premieres at the Court Theatre in Weimar
US PresidentAbraham Lincolnoutlaws business with Confederate States (US Civil War)
DrDavid Livingstone's body arrives in Southampton
Paul Krugerchosen as President of Transvaal
Harriet Quimbybecomes the 1st female pilot to fly across English Channel
Leninarrives back from exile in Russia at Finland Station, Petrograd to join the Russian Revolution [OS Apr 3]
Annie Oakleysets women's record by breaking 100 clay targets in a row
Short film "The Music Box" is released in the US, starringStan LaurelandOliver Hardy(1932 Academy Award for Best Live Action Short Film)
40-year-old future Baseball Hall of Fame sluggerBabe Ruthdebuts in the NL with a HR and single in Boston Braves 4-2 win over the NY Giants, in Boston
The Soviet Union proposes an alliance with Britain and France to counter Nazi Germany; the Soviets would later sign a secret agreement with the Nazis
Cleveland Indians pitcherBob Fellerhurls the first and only Opening Day no-hitter in MLB history; beats Chicago WSWhite Sox, 1-0 at Comiskey Park
Swiss scientist Dr.Albert Hofmanndiscovers the psychedelic effects of LSD
Nazis led byKlaus Barbieraid Izieu Jewish children's home, near Lyon, taking 44 children and seven adults to exterminations camps
Mel Ottplaying in right field for NY Giants hits his 511th and final MLB home run in 8-4 Opening Day win over Philadelphia Phillies
British royal yacht Britannia launched byQueen Elizabeth II
Walter Cronkitebegins anchoring CBS Evening News
Protestant UnionistIan Paisleywins seat formerly held byTerence O'Neillin the Stormont (North Ireland Parliament)
Alex Haleyfinds his Roots in Juffure, Gambia
Cardinals'Bob Forschno-hits Philadelphia Phillies, 5-0, in St. Louis
Arthur Asheretires from professional tennis
Steve Garveysets NL record by playing in 1,118 consecutive games
Oakland A'sDave Kingmanhits 3 HRs including a grand slam
To dispel rumors he's dead, Libyan leaderMuammar Gaddafiappears on TV
August Wilson's "Fences" wins 1987 Pulitzer Prize for Drama
Michael Jordanbecomes the second NBA player in history to score 3,000 points in a season
1st The Tradition Senior Men's Golf, GC at Desert Mountain: Don Bies wins his lone career Champions Tour major title by 1 stroke fromGary Player
David Lee Rotharrested in NYC for purchasing marijuana for $10
Anti-Records releases "Mule Variations", the thirteenth studio album by singer-songwriterTom Waits; his biggest selling album goes on to win Grammy Award for Best Contemporary Folk Album
Colin Farrellreaches a confidential settlement with ex-girlfriend Nicole Narain to keep their 13-minute sex tape private
Start of Papal Journey of PopeBenedict XVIto the United States
Laureus World Sports Awards, (ceremony cancelled GFC): Sportsman:Usain Bolt; Sportswoman: Yelena Isinbayeva; Team: China Olympic team
Elizabeth Holmes, American entrepreneur, inventor, and founder and CEO of Theranos, is named one of TIME's "100 Most Influential People" of 2015
Turkish PresidentRecep Tayyip Erdoğanwins referendum on 18-article constitutional reform package
World record for gathering ofCharlie Chaplinlookalikes - 662 at Manoir de Ban, Chaplin's World in Vevey, Switzerland
Kendrick Lamaris the first rapper and non-classical or jazz musician to win the Pulitzer Prize for music with his album "Damn"
Seattle Seahawks quarterbackRussell Wilsonbecomes the highest-paid player in NFL history with a 4-year $140m extension including record $65m signing bonus
Gary Sinisehosts the inauguration ceremony of the World War I memorial in Washington D.C.
Raúl Castroconfirms he is resigning as Cuban Communist Party leader, ending his family's six decade leadership of Cuba
Battle of Megiddo: Egyptian forces of Thutmose III defeat a large Canaanite coalition under the King of Kadesh. First battle recorded with a reliable account.
Battle of Berlin: Red Army begins its attack on the capital of Nazi Germany
Jury reaches guilty verdict in federal case against police officers (two convicted, two acquitted) who beatRodney King, but the verdict is not read until April 17th
John II, the Good, King of France (1350-64), born in Le Mans, France (d. 1364)
Petrus Apianus [Peter Apian], German mathematician, astronomer and cartographer of "Astronomicum Caesareum", born in Leisnig, Saxony (d. 1552)

Frans van Mieris the Elder, Dutch painter, born in Leiden, Netherlands (d. 1681)

John Luyken, Dutch poet and etcher (Duytse Lyre), born in Amsterdam (d. 1712)

John Hadley, English mathematician and inventor (octant), born in Bloomsbury, London (d. 1744)

Johann Gottlieb Gorner, German composer, born in Penig, Electorate of Saxony, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1778)
Joseph Black, Scottish chemist and physicist known for his discoveries of magnesium, latent heat and carbon dioxide, born in Bordeaux, France (d. 1799)
Henry Clinton, British army officer and politician, born in Newfoundland, British Canada (d. 1795)
Élisabeth-Louise Vigée Le Brun, French painter, born in Paris (d. 1842)
John Franklin, English rear admiral and explorer, who led and perished in expedition to find a Northwest Passage, born in Spilsby, Lincolnshire, England (d. 1847)
George Bingham, 3rd Earl of Lucan, British army officer (Charge of the Light Brigade), born in London, England (d. 1888)
Jozef Stefani, Polish composer, born in Warsaw, Poland (d. 1876)

Edward Johnson, American Major General (Confederate Army), born in Midlothian, Virginia (d. 1873)
Georg Curtius, German classical linguist, born in the Free Imperial City of Lübeck (d. 1885)

Ferdinand Eisenstein, German mathematician (number theory), born in Berlin, Prussia (d. 1852)

Orlando Bolivar Wilcox, American Brevet Major General (Union Army), born in Detroit, Michigan (d. 1907)
Edward Anson, British army officer, and administrator (Lieutenant Governor of Penang, 1867-82), born in London (d. 1925)

Karel Bendl, Czech composer, born in Prague, Kingdom of Bohemia, Austrian Empire (d. 1897)
Anatole France [Jacques Anatole Thibault], French writer (Thaïs, Wickerwork Woman, Nobel 1921), born in Paris (d. 1924)
Hans Auer, Swiss-Austrian architect, born in Wädenswil, Switerland (d. 1906)

Herbert Baxter Adams, American historian (American Historical Association), born in Shutesbury, Massachusetts (d. 1901)
Ernst Josephson, Swedish painter, born in Stockholm, Sweden (d. 1906)
Winifred Cochrane, Countess of Dundonald, Welsh philanthropist (Order of Saint John), born in Gwrych Castle, Wales (d. 1924)
Isaac Murphy, American jockey (won 628 races), born in Clark County, Kentucky (d. 1896)
Harry Chauvel, Australian Imperial Force senior officer, born in Tabulam, New South Wales, Australia (d. 1945)
José de Diego, Puerto Rican patriot and advocate for Puerto Rico's independence from Spain and the US, born in Aguadilla, Puerto Rico (d. 1918)
Spottiswoode Aitken, Scottish actor (The Birth of a Nation, The Avenging Conscience), born in Edinburgh, Scotland (d. 1933)
John Millington Synge, Irish dramatist and poet (Riders to the Sea), born in Rathfarnham, County Dublin, Ireland (d. 1909)
Martin Lunssens, Belgian composer, born in Sint-Jans-Molenbeek, Belgium (d. 1944)

R. E. Foster, English cricket batsman (8 Tests, 1 x 100, HS 287 on debut 1903; Worcestershire CCC) and soccer forward (5 caps; Corinthian FC, Old Malvernians FC; only man to captain England in both sports), born in Malvern, England (d. 1914)
Edward Frederick Wood, 1st Earl of Halifax and British ambassador to US (1940-46), born in Powderham Castle, Devon, England (d. 1959)
Seth Bingham, American organist and composer (Six Pieces for Organ), born in Bloomfield, New Jersey (d. 1972)

Karel Albert, Belgian composer (Marieken van Nymeghen), born in Antwerp, Belgium (d. 1987)
Paul Waner, American Baseball HOF right fielder (NL MVP 1927; NL batting champion 1927, 34, 36; MLB All-Star 1933–35, 37; Pittsburgh Pirates), born in Harrah, Oklahoma (d. 1965)
Clifford P. Case, American lawyer and politician, U.S. House of Representatives from New Jersey, born in Franklin Park, New Jersey (d. 1982)
Elizabeth Ann Duncan, American murderer, last woman to be executed in California before Furman v. Georgia, born in Kansas City, Missouri (d. 1962)
Fifi D'Orsay [Yvonne Lussier] Canadian-American vaudeville and film actress and singer known as the "French Bombshell" (The Life Jimmy Dolan; Dixie Jamboree), born in Montreal, Quebec (d. 1983)
Frits Philips, Dutch engineer and CEO (Philips), born in Eindhoven, Netherlands (d. 2005)
John Lee-Barber, British naval officer and commander of the Griffin destroyer during World War Ⅱ, born in England (d. 1995)

Herman Uyttersprot, Flemish literature historian, born in Denderbelle (d. 1967)

Constance Shacklock, English opera singer, born in Sherwood, Nottinghamshire (d. 1999)

John Hodiak, American actor (Battleground, A Bell for Adamo, Lifeboat), born in Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania (d. 1955)
Dany Tuijnman, Dutch politician (Minister of Transport and Water Management), born in Middelburg, Netherlands (d. 1992)

Joan Alexander [Louise Abrass], American actress (The New Adventures of Superman, The Tales of Hoffmann), born in St. Paul, Minnesota (d. 2009)

Tom Willmore, English geometer, born in Gillingham, England (d. 2005)

Kingsley Amis, English novelist (Lucky Jim; James Bond Dossier), born in London, England (d. 1995)

Bennie Green, American swing jazz and soul trombonist (Earl Hines), born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 1977)
Stewart Adams, English pharmacologist (developed Ibuprofen), born in Byfield, England (d. 2019)[1]

Geoffrey Johnson Smith, Scottish Conservative politician (1959-2001), born in Glasgow, Lanarkshire, Scotland (d. 2010)
John Harvey-Jones, English businessman and chairman of ICI (1982-87), born in Hackney, London (d. 2008)
Rudy Pompilli [Rudolph Pompilii], American jazz and rock saxophonist (Bill Haley and His Comets - "'Rudi's Rock"), born in Chester, Pennsylvania (d. 1976)

Edie Adams [Edith Enke], American businesswoman, singer, actress and comedian (Murial Cigar), born in Kingston, Pennsylvania (d. 2008)
Peter Mark Richman {Marvin Jack Richman], American character actor (Three's Company - "Rev. Snow"; Dynasty - "Andrew"), born in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania (d. 2021)
Dick 'Night Train' Lane, American Pro Football HOF cornerback (7 x Pro Bowl; 7 x First-team All-Pro; LA Rams, Chicago Cardinals, Detroit Lions), born in Austin, Texas (d. 2002)
Walt Dickerson, American jazz vibraphone player, and composer ("To My Queen"), born in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania (d. 2008)[1]
Ed Townsend, American doo-wop singer, songwriter, and producer ("For Your Love"; Marvin Gaye's "Let's Get It On"), born in Fayetteville, Tennessee (d. 2003)
Roy Hamilton, American singer ("You'll Never Walk Alone"), born in Leesburg, Georgia (d. 1969)

John Littlejohn [Funchess], American electric blues slide guitarist ("So-Called Friends"), born in Lake, Mississippi (d. 1994)
Leo Bersani, American literary critic and scholar known for his writings on the AIDS crisis (Homos), born in the Bronx, New York (d. 2022)[1]
Imre Polyák, Hungarian featherweight (Olympic gold 1964), born in Kecskemét, Hungary (d. 2010)
Vince Hill, English pop singer ("Roses of Picardy"; "La Vie en Rose"; "Edelweiss"), born in Holbrooks, Coventry, England (d. 2023)
Joan Bakewell, British broadcaster and actress (Cold Comfort Farm), born in Stockport, England
Perry Botkin Jr, American arranger, composer ("Nadia's Theme"), and producer (Incredible Bongo Band), born in New York City (d. 2021)
Geoffrey Owen, British journalist and editor of the Financial Times (1981-90), born in England
Richard Kenshaw, British broadcaster, born in London (d. 2014)

Robert Stigwood, Australian-British music entrepreneur (RSO Records), stage (Jesus Christ Superstar) and film (Saturday Night Fever; Grease; Sgt. Pepper's Lonely Hearts Club Band) producer, born in Adelaide, South Australia (d. 2016)
